text: four years ago we really thought we

here today we had experienced and heard

discrimination and stereotyping and we

nationwide epidemic that we ourselves
spread and now can't seem to recognize
or get rid of we're not there yet today
we are here to raise our standards of

everywhere across the United States for
our youngest and future generations to
grow up equipped with the tools to
understand navigate and improve a world

others experiences as if they were our

of which are racially divided if you
don't go searching for an education

when we did have conversations about

first the heart gap an inability to
understand
and each of our experiences too fiercely

it might even be annoying at some points

throwing out just the statistic just the

today for us sometimes we would talk

experiences in the classroom stuff like
how Justin once told us I've been
working on psychologically reclaiming my

my Chicago isn't the nice architecture

Chicago is the orange line the pink line
the working immigrant class going on the

isolated experiences another example
Sandra in DC once told us when I'm with

them feel like I care about them and
making and sharing food is one of the
most fundamental ways of showing love
when I'm with my partner who's not

the fact that I'm very food centric and
he's just not one time he said that he
didn't want to be expected to make food
for me
I got really upset that might seem like
a weird reaction but only if we don't
recognize how it's emblematic of
something larger something deeper
intergenerational trauma how in Sandra's
family widespread hunger and poverty
existed as recently as Sandra's parents
generation and therefore impact Sandra
today she experiences someone saying I
don't want to feed you as I don't want
to hug you and without her and her
partner having that nuanced
understanding of her reaction and the
historical context behind it it could
easily lead to unnecessary fighting
that's why it's so important that we
proactively co-create a shared American

diverse communities to be racially
literate to understand who we are so
that we can heal together we cannot
